Credex · credex.rocks 
Welcome 
Thanks for applying to Credex. Because of the volume of applications, our hiring 
process is structured as: 
1. Round 1 (this document)  — 7-day take-home build. AI-assisted review at first 
pass, human review for shortlisted submissions. 2. Round 2  — A shorter, focused build (~2 days). Released within 3 working days 
of the Round 1 deadline, only to candidates who clear Round 1. 3. Interviews  — 1–2 conversations with the team, including a code walkthrough. Because we use AI to review submissions at the first stage, the format of your 
deliverable matters as much as the work itself  . Follow the file naming and structure 
exactly. Submissions that don’t follow the format are filtered out before any human sees 
them. This is not a coding assignment. It’s an entrepreneurial assignment that requires 
shipping working code. We’re hiring people who can ship a product, not people who can 
solve a Leetcode problem.

The Project — AI Spend Audit 
The opportunity (read this carefully)  
Credex sells discounted AI infrastructure credits — Cursor, Claude, ChatGPT 
Enterprise, and others — sourced from companies that overforecast or pivoted. The 
discount is real and substantial. But here’s the problem: most startups don’t know they’re overspending on AI tools 
in the first place. They look at their monthly bill, sigh, and pay it. They have no 
benchmark, no obvious alternatives surfaced, no second opinion. There’s no “Mint for AI 
tool spend.” 
Your project is to build that tool. It’s a free product that genuinely helps any startup 
founder or engineering manager who uses it — and it’s a lead-generation asset for 
Credex, because the audits will surface real overspend, and for users with significant 
savings opportunities, Credex is the solution. You are not building a coding exercise. You are building something Credex could 
plausibly launch on Product Hunt next month. Treat it that way.

You are not building a coding exercise. You are building something Credex could 
plausibly launch on Product Hunt next month. Treat it that way. CONFIDENTIAL  
What you’re building  
A free web app — call it whatever you want, naming is part of the test — that does this 
end-toend: 
1. A cold visitor lands on the page from a tweet, a blog post, or Hacker News 
2. They input what AI tools they pay for, what plan, monthly spend, team size, and 
primary use case 
3. They get an instant on-screen audit: where they’re overspending, what to switch 
to or downgrade, and total potential monthly + annual savings 
4. They get an option to capture the report (email gate) and — for high-savings 
cases — book a Credex consultation 
5. The result is shareable via a unique public URL with proper Open Graph 
previews 
No login required to use the tool. Email is captured after value is shown, never before. MVP features (all six required)

Q1. Explain Perceptron with its Parts. Write Two Applications and One

Introduction  
A Perceptron  is one of the earliest and simplest types of artificial neural networks. It 
was introduced in 1958 by Frank Rosenblatt . The perceptron is mainly used for solving 
binary classification problems , where the output belongs to one of two classes (0 or 
1, Yes or No, True or False). It is considered the basic building block of modern neural networks. Structure and Parts of Perceptron  
 
 
4 
A perceptron consists of the following main components:  
1. Input Layer (x₁, x₂, x₃, …, x ₙ) 
These are the input features given to the model. For example, in spam detection, inputs 
may be frequency of certain words. 2. Weights (w₁, w₂, w₃, …, w ₙ) 
Each input has a corresponding weight. Weights determine the importance of each input feature. If a weight is large, that input strongly influences the output. 3. Bias (b)  
Bias is an additional parameter added to shift the decision boundary.

If a weight is large, that input strongly influences the output. 3. Bias (b)  
Bias is an additional parameter added to shift the decision boundary. It helps the model fit data better. 4. Summation Unit  
The perceptron calculates a weighted sum:  
z=w1x1+w2x2+...+wnxn+b  
This value is called the net input . 5. Activation Function (Step Function)  
After calculating z, a step function is applied:  
Output={  
1, if z≥0  
0, if z<0  
} 
The activation function decides the final output. Working of Perceptron  
1. Take inputs. 2. Multiply each input with its weight. 3. Add all weighted values and bias. 4. Apply step activation function. 5. Produce binary output. Applications of Perceptron (Any Two)  
1. Spam Email Detection  
 Classifies emails as spam (1) or not spam (0). 2. Simple Pattern Recognition  
 Used to implement logical gates like AND and OR. 3. Binary Image Classification  
 Distinguishes between two categories of images.

2. Simple Pattern Recognition  
 Used to implement logical gates like AND and OR. 3. Binary Image Classification  
 Distinguishes between two categories of images. Limitation of Perceptron  
The major limitation of perceptron is:  
  It cannot solve non -linearly separable problems , such as the XOR problem. If the data cannot be separated by a straight line, perceptron fails. Q2. Explain the Structure of Neural

Dropout: How It Prevents Overfitting  
Introduction  
Dropout is a regularization technique  used in neural networks to reduce overfitting . Overfitting occurs when a model learns the training data too well (including noise and 
details) and performs poorly on new, unseen data. Dropout solves this problem by randomly deactivating some neurons during training . Diagram Explanation (Before vs After Dropout)  
1. Without Dropout (Normal Neural Network)  
 
 
 
6 
• All neurons are active  
• Every neuron is connected to others  
• The network may learn very complex and specific patterns  
• This leads to overfitting   
 
2.

With Dropout  
 
 
 
6 
• Some neurons are randomly turned OFF during training  
• Their connections are temporarily removed  
• Each training step uses a slightly different network  
 
How Dropout Works  
1. During training:  
a. A fraction of neurons is randomly selected  
b. These neurons are temporarily deactivated  
c. This is controlled by a probability (e.g., dropout rate = 0.5)  
2. During testing:  
a. All neurons are active  
b. Outputs are scaled appropriately  
 
How Dropout Prevents Overfitting  
1. Prevents Co -adaptation of Neurons  
• In a normal network, neurons depend on each other  
• With dropout, neurons cannot rely on specific other neurons  
• Each neuron must learn useful features independently  
 
2. Acts Like Training Multiple Networks  
Dropout behaves like training many smaller neural networks:  
• Each iteration uses a different subset of neurons  
• These networks share weights  
• Final result is similar to ensemble learning   
This improves model robustness. 3.

3. Improves Generalization  
• The model avoids memorizing training data  
• Learns more general patterns  
• Performs better on unseen data  
 
Simple Example  
Suppose a layer has 6 neurons:  
• Without dropout → all 6 neurons are active  
• With dropout (rate = 0.5) → only ~3 neurons are active each time  
Each training iteration uses a different combination → better learning. Advantages of Dropout  
1. Reduces overfitting  
2. Improves generalization  
3. Easy to implement  
4. Works well for large neural networks  
 
Disadvantages  
• Training becomes slightly slower  
• Too much dropout can cause underfitting    
Conclusion (Exam Ready)  
Dropout is an effective regularization technique in neural networks where a subset of 
neurons is randomly deactivated during training. This prevents neurons from becoming 
overly dependent on each other, reduces overfitting, and improves the model’s ability  to 
generalize to new data. Q.

gradient problem in RNN? (6 Marks)  
 
Introduction  
Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) are used for sequential data, but they suffer from 
the vanishing gradient problem , where gradients become very small during 
backpropagation. Because of this, the network fails to learn long -term dependencies . To solve this problem, LSTM (Long Short -Term Memory)  networks were introduced. Vanishing Gradient Problem in RNN  
• In RNN, gradients are repeatedly multiplied during backpropagation through 
time (BPTT). • After many steps, gradients become very small (close to zero). • As a result:  
o Earlier information is forgotten  
o Long -term dependencies are not learned  
 LSTM Architecture  
 
 
 
5 
LSTM introduces a memory cell  and gates  to control information flow. Main Components of LSTM  
1.

Main Components of LSTM  
1. Cell State (Memory)  
• Acts like a conveyor belt  
• Carries information across time steps  
• Allows constant error flow  
 
2. Forget Gate  
• Decides what information to remove  
• Uses sigmoid function (0 to 1)  
 
3. Input Gate  
• Decides what new information to store  
• Controls updates to memory  
 
4. Output Gate  
• Decides what information to output  
 
How LSTM Solves Vanishing Gradient  
1. Constant Error Flow  
• Cell state allows gradients to pass unchanged  
• Prevents gradients from becoming zero  
 
2. Controlled Information Flow  
• Gates regulate what to keep, forget, and output  
• Avoids unnecessary information  
 
3. Long -Term Memory  
• Important information is stored for long duration  
• Enables learning of long dependencies  
 
Key Idea  
     Instead of repeatedly multiplying gradients (like RNN),  
 LSTM allows direct flow of information through cell state , reducing gradient loss.

Conclusion  
LSTM overcomes the vanishing gradient problem by introducing a memory cell and 
gating mechanisms that allow stable gradient flow and long -term information retention. Q. Explain the role of Activation Function in
